docs.title: A Modular, Extensible FastAPI Framework
docs.text: Apiary is a production-ready FastAPI framework designed for building modular, extensible REST APIs with minimal code. It provides a robust foundation with authentication, rate limiting, metrics, health checks, and a unique configuration-driven endpoint system.

docs.title: Production-Ready
docs.text:
• Structured logging with correlation IDs
  • Rate limiting with configurable limits
  • Health checks (liveness and readiness probes)
  • Metrics collection for monitoring
  • Request validation and size limits
  • Caching support with cache headers

docs.title: Security
docs.text:
• API key authentication with flexible authorization
  • Security headers middleware
  • CORS support for cross-origin requests
  • Input sanitization and validation

docs.title: Modular & Extensible
docs.text:
• Configuration-driven endpoints - add endpoints without code changes
  • Service-based architecture with dependency injection
  • Plugin system for custom services
  • Endpoint discovery API

docs.title: Developer Experience
docs.text:
• Type-safe configuration using Pydantic Settings
  • Comprehensive error handling with standardized responses
  • Auto-generated API documentation (Swagger UI & ReDoc)
  • Testing utilities with mocks and fixtures
  • Code quality tools (black, ruff, mypy)

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
# Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/lancereinsmith/apiary.git
cd apiary

# Install dependencies
uv sync

# Create configuration
cp settings_template.json settings.json
cp config/endpoints_template.json config/endpoints.json

# Run the application
python main.py

Visit http://localhost:8000/docs for interactive API documentation.

docs.title: Configuration-Driven Endpoints
docs.text:
Add new API endpoints without writing code - just edit a JSON configuration file:

{
  "endpoints": [
    {
      "path": "/api/crypto",
      "method": "GET",
      "service": "crypto",
      "enabled": true,
      "requires_auth": false,
      "description": "Get cryptocurrency price data"
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Service-Based Architecture
docs.text:
Create reusable services that can be called by multiple endpoints:

from core.services.base import BaseService

class MyService(BaseService):
    async def call(self, parameters=None):
        # Your business logic here
        return {"result": "data"}

Register the service and use it in configurable endpoints or code-based routes.

docs.title: Built-in Production Features
docs.text:
• Rate Limiting: Per-endpoint limits with different rates for authenticated users
  • Metrics: Track request counts, response times, and error rates
  • Health Checks: Kubernetes-compatible liveness and readiness probes
  • Caching: Response caching with configurable TTLs
  • Logging: Structured logging with correlation IDs

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
Apiary is perfect for:

  • Personal APIs: Quickly build APIs for personal projects
  • Microservices: Create modular, maintainable microservices
  • API Gateways: Build custom API gateways with routing and aggregation
  • Rapid Prototyping: Test ideas with configuration-driven endpoints
  • Learning: Study production-ready FastAPI architecture

docs.title: Architecture Overview
docs.text:
apiary/
├── config/              # Configuration management
│   ├── settings.py      # Pydantic Settings
│   └── endpoint_config.py  # Endpoint loader
├── core/                # Core utilities
│   ├── auth/            # Authentication & authorization
│   ├── services/        # Base service classes
│   ├── exceptions.py    # Custom exceptions
│   ├── middleware.py    # Custom middleware
│   ├── rate_limiter.py  # Rate limiting
│   ├── metrics.py       # Metrics collection
│   └── cache.py         # Caching utilities
├── routers/             # API route handlers
├── services/            # Business logic services
├── models/              # Request/response models
├── templates/           # HTML templates
└── main.py              # Application entry point

docs.title: Contributing to Apiary
docs.text: Thank you for your interest in contributing to Apiary!

docs.title: Quick Contribution Guide
docs.text:
1. Fork the repository
  2. Create a feature branch: git checkout -b feature/amazing-feature
  3. Make your changes following our code style
  4. Run tests: pytest
  5. Format code: black .
  6. Lint code: ruff check .
  7. Commit and push your changes
  8. Open a Pull Request

docs.title: Development Setup
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/lancereinsmith/apiary.git
cd apiary
uv sync
cp settings_template.json settings.json
python main.py

docs.title: Code Style
docs.text:
• Use type hints for all function parameters and return values
  • Write docstrings for modules and functions
  • Keep functions focused and single-purpose
  • Maximum line length: 100 characters
  • Follow existing code patterns

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
from typing import Optional
from pydantic import BaseModel


class UserRequest(BaseModel):
    """Request model for user operations."""
    username: str
    email: Optional[str] = None


async def process_user(user: UserRequest) -> dict[str, str]:
    """Process a user request.

    Args:
        user: The user request data

    Returns:
        Dictionary containing the processing result
    """
    if not user.username:
        raise ValueError("Username cannot be empty")

    return {"status": "success", "username": user.username}

docs.title: Testing
docs.text:
Write tests for all new features:

import pytest
from fastapi.testclient import TestClient


def test_endpoint_success(client: TestClient):
    """Test that the endpoint returns success for valid input."""
    response = client.get("/api/endpoint")
    assert response.status_code == 200
    assert response.json()["status"] == "success"

Run tests with:

pytest              # All tests
pytest --cov        # With coverage
pytest tests/unit/  # Unit tests only

docs.title: Deployment Configuration
docs.text: Configure Apiary for production deployment.

docs.title: Production Settings
docs.text:
{
  "api_keys": "strong-random-key-1,strong-random-key-2",
  "enable_landing_page": true,
  "rate_limit_enabled": true,
  "rate_limit_per_minute": 60,
  "rate_limit_per_minute_authenticated": 300
}

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text:
export API_KEYS="prod-key-1,prod-key-2"
export RATE_LIMIT_ENABLED=true

docs.title: Security
docs.text:
1. Use strong API keys (32+ characters)
  2. Enable HTTPS with SSL/TLS
  3. Restrict file permissions: chmod 600 settings.json
  4. Use environment variables for secrets
  5. Enable rate limiting

docs.text:
# In systemd service file
ExecStart=gunicorn -w 4 -k uvicorn.workers.UvicornWorker main:api

Workers = (2 × CPU cores) + 1

docs.title: Server Setup
docs.text: Deploy Apiary to a server with nginx and systemd.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Ubuntu 20.04+ or Debian 11+ server
  • Python 3.11+
  • nginx
  • systemd

docs.title: 1. Install Dependencies
docs.text:
sudo apt update
sudo apt install python3 python3-venv python3-pip nginx

# Install uv
curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh

docs.title: 2. Clone and Setup
docs.text:
cd ~
git clone <your-repo-url> apiary
cd apiary

uv sync

cp settings_template.json settings.json
cp config/endpoints_template.json config/endpoints.json

# Edit with production values
nano settings.json

docs.title: 3. Configure nginx
docs.text:
Copy nginx configuration:

sudo cp _server/nginx/api.domain.com.nginx /etc/nginx/sites-available/apiary
sudo ln -s /etc/nginx/sites-available/apiary /etc/nginx/sites-enabled/

# Edit and update paths
sudo nano /etc/nginx/sites-available/apiary

# Test and reload
sudo nginx -t
sudo systemctl reload nginx

docs.title: 4. Configure systemd
docs.text:
sudo cp _server/units/api.domain.com.service /etc/systemd/system/apiary.service

# Edit and update paths
sudo nano /etc/systemd/system/apiary.service

# Enable and start
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl enable apiary
sudo systemctl start apiary
sudo systemctl status apiary

docs.title: 5. SSL/TLS (Recommended)
docs.text:
sudo apt install certbot python3-certbot-nginx
sudo certbot --nginx -d yourdomain.com

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:
# Check service
sudo systemctl status apiary

# Check logs
sudo journalctl -u apiary -f

# Test endpoint
curl https://yourdomain.com/health

docs.title: CLI Reference
docs.text: Apiary includes a comprehensive command-line interface (CLI) for common maintenance and development tasks. All commands are accessed through a single apiary entry point with various subcommands.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The CLI is built with Click and provides a consistent interface for:

  • Running tests and generating coverage reports
  • Code formatting and linting
  • Type checking
  • Building and deploying documentation
  • Starting the development server
  • Cleaning up generated files

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
The CLI is automatically installed when you install Apiary:

uv sync

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
All commands follow this pattern:

uv run apiary [OPTIONS] COMMAND [ARGS]...

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
# Show all available commands
uv run apiary --help

# Show help for a specific command
uv run apiary test --help
uv run apiary docs-serve --help

# Show version
uv run apiary --version

docs.title: test
docs.text:
Run the test suite using pytest.

Usage:

uv run apiary test [OPTIONS]

Options:

  • -v, --verbose - Enable verbose output
  • -c, --coverage - Run tests with coverage report

Examples:

# Run all tests
uv run apiary test

# Run with verbose output
uv run apiary test -v

# Run with coverage
uv run apiary test --coverage

docs.title: coverage
docs.text:
Generate a detailed coverage report with optional HTML output.

Usage:

uv run apiary coverage [OPTIONS]

Options:

  • --html - Generate HTML coverage report (default: true)
  • -o, --open-browser - Open the HTML report in browser after generation

Examples:

# Generate coverage report
uv run apiary coverage

# Generate and open in browser
uv run apiary coverage -o

The HTML report is generated in htmlcov/index.html.

docs.title: format-code
docs.text:
Format Python code using Black.

Usage:

uv run apiary format-code [OPTIONS]

Options:

  • --check - Check formatting without modifying files

Examples:

# Format all Python files
uv run apiary format-code

# Check formatting without changes
uv run apiary format-code --check

docs.title: lint
docs.text:
Run the Ruff linter to check for code quality issues.

Usage:

uv run apiary lint [OPTIONS]

Options:

  • --fix - Automatically fix issues where possible

Examples:

# Check for linting issues
uv run apiary lint

# Fix issues automatically
uv run apiary lint --fix

docs.title: typecheck
docs.text:
Run MyPy for static type checking.

Usage:

uv run apiary typecheck

Example:

uv run apiary typecheck

docs.title: check-all
docs.text:
Run all code quality checks in sequence: formatting, linting, type checking, and tests.

Usage:

uv run apiary check-all [OPTIONS]

Options:

  • --fix - Automatically fix issues where possible (for format and lint)

Examples:

# Run all checks
uv run apiary check-all

# Run all checks and auto-fix issues
uv run apiary check-all --fix

This command provides a summary at the end showing which checks passed or failed:

Summary:
===============================================================
Format          ✅ PASSED
Lint            ✅ PASSED
Type Check      ✅ PASSED
Tests           ✅ PASSED

🎉 All checks passed!

docs.title: docs-serve
docs.text:
Serve documentation locally using MkDocs.

Usage:

uv run apiary docs-serve [OPTIONS]

Options:

  • -p, --port INTEGER - Port to serve on (default: 8000)

Examples:

# Serve on default port (8000)
uv run apiary docs-serve

# Serve on custom port
uv run apiary docs-serve -p 8001

Visit http://127.0.0.1:8000 (or your custom port) to view the documentation.

docs.title: docs-build
docs.text:
Build the documentation site to the site/ directory.

Usage:

uv run apiary docs-build

Example:

uv run apiary docs-build

The built documentation will be in the site/ directory.

docs.title: docs-deploy
docs.text:
Deploy documentation to GitHub Pages.

Usage:

uv run apiary docs-deploy [OPTIONS]

Options:

  • -m, --message TEXT - Custom deployment commit message

Examples:

# Deploy with default message
uv run apiary docs-deploy

# Deploy with custom message
uv run apiary docs-deploy -m "Update API reference"

This command:

  1. Builds the documentation
  2. Pushes it to the gh-pages branch
  3. GitHub Pages automatically publishes it

docs.title: serve
docs.text:
Start the Apiary API server.

Usage:

uv run apiary serve [HOST] [PORT] [OPTIONS]

Arguments:

  • HOST - Host address (default: 127.0.0.1)
  • PORT - Port number (default: 8000)

Options:

  • --reload - Enable auto-reload for development

Examples:

# Start on default host/port
uv run apiary serve

# Start on custom host and port
uv run apiary serve 0.0.0.0 8080

# Start with auto-reload (development)
uv run apiary serve --reload

docs.title: clean
docs.text:
Remove generated files and caches to clean up your workspace.

Usage:

uv run apiary clean

What it removes:

  • __pycache__ directories
  • .pyc and .pyo files
  • .pytest_cache
  • .mypy_cache
  • .ruff_cache
  • htmlcov (coverage reports)
  • .coverage files
  • site (built documentation)
  • *.egg-info directories

Example:

uv run apiary clean

docs.title: Before Committing
docs.text:
Run all checks to ensure your changes are ready:

uv run apiary check-all --fix

docs.title: Development Loop
docs.text:
While developing, use auto-reload:

uv run apiary serve --reload

docs.title: Documentation Updates
docs.text:
1. Make changes to markdown files in docs/
  2. Preview locally:
uv run apiary docs-serve
  1. Deploy when ready:
uv run apiary docs-deploy

docs.title: Testing with Coverage
docs.text:
# Run tests with coverage
uv run apiary coverage

# Open the HTML report
uv run apiary coverage -o

docs.title: Continuous Integration
docs.text:
For CI pipelines, run all checks without auto-fixing:

uv run apiary check-all

docs.title: Exit Codes
docs.text:
All commands return standard exit codes:

  • 0 - Success
  • Non-zero - Failure

This makes them suitable for use in scripts and CI/CD pipelines.

docs.title: Output Formatting
docs.text:
The CLI uses colored output for better readability:

  • 🔧 Blue - Command running
  • ✅ Green - Success
  • ❌ Red - Failure
  • 📊 - Informational messages

docs.title: Extending the CLI
docs.text:
To add new commands, edit cli.py and add a new Click command:

@cli.command()
@click.option('--option', help='An option')
def my_command(option):
    """My custom command."""
    run_command(['my-tool', '--flag'], 'Running my tool')

Then rebuild the package:

uv sync

Your new command will be available as:

uv run apiary my-command

docs.title: Code Quality
docs.text: Maintain high code quality in Apiary.

docs.title: Quick Commands
docs.text:
black .              # Format code
ruff check .         # Lint
ruff check --fix .   # Auto-fix lint issues
mypy .               # Type check
pytest --cov         # Test with coverage

docs.title: Type Hints
docs.text:
Always use type hints:

async def process_data(data: dict[str, Any]) -> dict[str, str]:
    """Process data and return result."""
    return {"status": "success"}

docs.title: Docstrings
docs.text:
Document public APIs:

def calculate_rate_limit(requests: int, window: int) -> float:
    """Calculate rate limit per second.

    Args:
        requests: Number of allowed requests
        window: Time window in seconds

    Returns:
        Requests per second as float
    """
    return requests / window

docs.title: Apiary Patterns
docs.text:
Follow existing patterns:

  • Services inherit from BaseService
  • Use dependency injection for HTTP clients and settings
  • Raise APIException for business logic errors
  • Use structured logging with correlation IDs
  • Return Pydantic models or dicts from endpoints

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
from core.exceptions import APIException

def validate_input(value: str) -> str:
    """Validate input value."""
    if not value:
        raise APIException(
            status_code=400,
            error="INVALID_INPUT",
            message="Value cannot be empty"
        )
    return value

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Tool configurations are in pyproject.toml:

  • Black: Line length 100, Python 3.11+
  • Ruff: Selected rules for modern Python
  • Mypy: Strict mode enabled
  • Pytest: Coverage reporting configured

docs.title: Pre-commit Checks
docs.text:
Optional pre-commit hooks:

pip install pre-commit
pre-commit install
pre-commit run --all-files

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Format before committing - Always run black .
  2. Fix linter warnings - Run ruff check --fix .
  3. Add type hints - All function signatures
  4. Write tests - Maintain >80% coverage
  5. Document public APIs - Clear docstrings
  6. Follow patterns - Match existing code style

docs.title: Creating a New Service
docs.text:
1. Create service file in services/
  2. Inherit from BaseService
  3. Implement async def call(self, parameters)
  4. Register in services/__init__.py

Example:

# services/weather_service.py
from core.services.base import BaseService

class WeatherService(BaseService):
    async def call(self, parameters=None):
        # Your logic here
        return {"temperature": 72}

# services/__init__.py
from core.services import register_service
from services.weather_service import WeatherService

register_service("weather", WeatherService)

docs.title: Adding Configuration-Based Endpoints
docs.text:
Edit config/endpoints.json:

{
  "path": "/api/weather",
  "method": "GET",
  "service": "weather",
  "enabled": true,
  "requires_auth": false
}

docs.title: Adding Code-Based Endpoints
docs.text:
Create a router in routers/:

# routers/custom.py
from fastapi import APIRouter

router = APIRouter(prefix="/custom", tags=["custom"])

@router.get("/")
async def custom_endpoint():
    return {"message": "Custom endpoint"}

Register in main.py:

from routers import custom
app.include_router(custom.router)

docs.title: Available Fixtures
docs.text:
From conftest.py:

  • client - TestClient for sync requests
  • async_client - AsyncClient for async tests
  • mock_settings - Mock settings object
  • mock_http_client - Mock HTTP client
  • auth_headers - Valid auth headers dict
  • no_auth_headers - Empty headers dict

docs.title: Unit Test for Services
docs.text:
import pytest
from services.crypto_service import CryptoService

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_crypto_service(mock_http_client):
    """Test crypto service returns expected data."""
    service = CryptoService(http_client=mock_http_client)
    result = await service.call({"symbol": "BTC"})

    assert result["symbol"] == "BTC"
    assert "price" in result

docs.text:
def test_health_endpoint(client):
    """Test health endpoint returns healthy status."""
    response = client.get("/health")

    assert response.status_code == 200
    assert response.json()["status"] == "healthy"

docs.title: Testing Authentication
docs.text:
def test_protected_endpoint_no_auth(client, no_auth_headers):
    """Test protected endpoint requires authentication."""
    response = client.get("/api/protected", headers=no_auth_headers)

    assert response.status_code == 401


def test_protected_endpoint_with_auth(client, auth_headers):
    """Test protected endpoint succeeds with valid auth."""
    response = client.get("/api/protected", headers=auth_headers)

    assert response.status_code == 200

docs.title: Testing Configurable Endpoints
docs.text:
def test_configurable_endpoint(client):
    """Test dynamically created endpoint."""
    response = client.get("/api/crypto")

    assert response.status_code == 200
    assert "symbol" in response.json()

docs.title: Testing New Services
docs.text:
When creating a new service, test:

  1. Success case - Valid input returns expected output
  2. Error handling - Invalid input raises appropriate errors
  3. Edge cases - Empty params, missing fields, etc.
  4. HTTP calls - Mock external API calls

Example:

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_weather_service_success(mock_http_client):
    """Test weather service with valid input."""
    service = WeatherService(http_client=mock_http_client)
    result = await service.call({"city": "Boston"})

    assert result["city"] == "Boston"
    assert "temperature" in result


@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_weather_service_missing_city(mock_http_client):
    """Test weather service handles missing city."""
    service = WeatherService(http_client=mock_http_client)

    with pytest.raises(APIException) as exc:
        await service.call({})

    assert exc.value.status_code == 400

pytest --cov --cov-report=html
open htmlcov/index.html  # View coverage report

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: Learn how to configure Apiary for your needs.

docs.title: Configuration Files
docs.text:
Apiary uses two main configuration files:

  1. settings.json - Application settings (API keys, rate limits, etc.)
  2. config/endpoints.json - Endpoint definitions

Both files are ignored by git (via .gitignore) to keep sensitive data secure.

docs.title: Creating Settings File
docs.text:
Copy the template to create your settings file:

cp settings_template.json settings.json

docs.title: Settings Structure
docs.text:
{
  "api_keys": "key1,key2,key3",
  "enable_landing_page": true,
  "rate_limit_enabled": true,
  "rate_limit_per_minute": 60,
  "rate_limit_per_minute_authenticated": 300
}

docs.title: api_keys
docs.text:
Comma-separated list of valid API keys for authentication.

{
  "api_keys": "secret-key-1,secret-key-2,secret-key-3"
}
  • Type: String (comma-separated)
  • Default: Empty string (no authentication)
  • Example: "key1,key2,key3"

Generating API Keys

Generate strong random API keys: 

python -c "import secrets; print(secrets.token_urlsafe(32))"

docs.title: enable_landing_page
docs.text:
Enable or disable the HTML landing page at /.

{
  "enable_landing_page": true
}
  • Type: Boolean
  • Default: true
  • Values: true (enabled), false (disabled)

docs.title: rate_limit_enabled
docs.text:
Enable or disable rate limiting globally.

{
  "rate_limit_enabled": true
}
  • Type: Boolean
  • Default: false
  • Values: true (enabled), false (disabled)

docs.title: rate_limit_per_minute
docs.text:
Rate limit for unauthenticated requests (requests per minute per IP).

{
  "rate_limit_per_minute": 60
}
  • Type: Integer
  • Default: 60
  • Example: 60 (60 requests per minute)

docs.title: rate_limit_per_minute_authenticated
docs.text:
Rate limit for authenticated requests (requests per minute per API key).

{
  "rate_limit_per_minute_authenticated": 300
}
  • Type: Integer
  • Default: 300
  • Example: 300 (300 requests per minute)

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text:
Settings can be overridden with environment variables:

export API_KEYS="key1,key2"
export RATE_LIMIT_ENABLED=true
export RATE_LIMIT_PER_MINUTE=100
export RATE_LIMIT_PER_MINUTE_AUTHENTICATED=500

python main.py

Environment variables take precedence over settings.json.

docs.title: Development
docs.text:
{
  "api_keys": "dev-key-123",
  "enable_landing_page": true,
  "rate_limit_enabled": false
}

docs.title: Production
docs.text:
{
  "api_keys": "prod-key-a1b2c3d4,prod-key-e5f6g7h8",
  "enable_landing_page": true,
  "rate_limit_enabled": true,
  "rate_limit_per_minute": 60,
  "rate_limit_per_minute_authenticated": 300
}

docs.title: Testing
docs.text:
{
  "api_keys": "test-key",
  "enable_landing_page": false,
  "rate_limit_enabled": false
}

docs.title: Creating Endpoints File
docs.text:
Copy the template:

cp config/endpoints_template.json config/endpoints.json

docs.title: Endpoint Structure
docs.text:
{
  "endpoints": [
    {
      "path": "/api/example",
      "method": "GET",
      "service": "example",
      "enabled": true,
      "requires_auth": false,
      "description": "Example endpoint",
      "tags": ["example"],
      "summary": "Example endpoint",
      "parameters": {
        "param1": {
          "source": "query",
          "key": "param1"
        }
      }
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: path
docs.text:
The URL path for the endpoint.

  • Type: String
  • Required: Yes
  • Example: "/api/crypto", "/api/hello"
  • Must start with: /

docs.title: method
docs.text:
HTTP method for the endpoint.

  • Type: String
  • Required: Yes
  • Values: "GET", "POST", "PUT", "DELETE", "PATCH"
  • Example: "GET"

docs.title: service
docs.text:
Name of the service to call (must be registered).

  • Type: String
  • Required: Yes
  • Example: "crypto", "hello"

docs.title: enabled
docs.text:
Enable or disable the endpoint.

  • Type: Boolean
  • Required: Yes
  • Values: true (enabled), false (disabled)
  • Example: true

docs.title: requires_auth
docs.text:
Whether authentication is required.

  • Type: Boolean
  • Required: Yes
  • Values: true (protected), false (public)
  • Example: false

docs.title: description
docs.text:
Detailed description for API documentation.

  • Type: String
  • Required: No
  • Example: "Get cryptocurrency price data. Accepts optional 'symbol' parameter."

docs.title: tags
docs.text:
OpenAPI tags for grouping endpoints.

  • Type: Array of strings
  • Required: No
  • Example: ["crypto", "finance"]

docs.title: summary
docs.text:
Short summary for API documentation.

  • Type: String
  • Required: No
  • Example: "Cryptocurrency prices"

docs.title: parameters
docs.text:
Parameter mapping from request to service.

  • Type: Object
  • Required: No

Parameter mapping types:

{
  "parameters": {
    "static_value": "hardcoded-value",
    "from_query": {
      "source": "query",
      "key": "q"
    },
    "from_path": {
      "source": "path",
      "key": "id"
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Simple Public Endpoint
docs.text:
{
  "path": "/api/crypto",
  "method": "GET",
  "service": "crypto",
  "enabled": true,
  "requires_auth": false,
  "description": "Get cryptocurrency prices",
  "tags": ["crypto"]
}

docs.title: Protected Endpoint
docs.text:
{
  "path": "/api/admin/stats",
  "method": "GET",
  "service": "stats",
  "enabled": true,
  "requires_auth": true,
  "description": "Admin statistics (requires API key)",
  "tags": ["admin"]
}

docs.title: Endpoint with Parameters
docs.text:
{
  "path": "/api/crypto",
  "method": "GET",
  "service": "crypto",
  "enabled": true,
  "requires_auth": false,
  "description": "Get crypto price by symbol",
  "tags": ["crypto"],
  "parameters": {
    "symbol": {
      "source": "query",
      "key": "symbol"
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Disabled Endpoint
docs.text:
{
  "path": "/api/beta-feature",
  "method": "GET",
  "service": "beta",
  "enabled": false,
  "requires_auth": false,
  "description": "Beta feature (currently disabled)"
}

docs.title: Priority Order
docs.text:
Configuration is loaded in this order (later overrides earlier):

  1. Default values in code
  2. settings.json file
  3. Environment variables

docs.title: Configuration Validation
docs.text:
Apiary validates configuration on startup:

  • ✅ Valid JSON syntax
  • ✅ Required fields present
  • ✅ Valid types (strings, booleans, integers)
  • ✅ Service names are registered
  • ✅ Endpoint paths start with /
  • ✅ HTTP methods are valid

If validation fails, the application will not start and will show an error message.

docs.title: Reloading Configuration
docs.text:
Configuration is loaded once at startup. To apply changes:

# Restart the server
# Press Ctrl+C to stop, then:
python main.py

For development, use auto-reload:

uvicorn main:api --reload

docs.title: Using Environment Files
docs.text:
Create a .env file for environment variables:

# .env
API_KEYS=key1,key2,key3
RATE_LIMIT_ENABLED=true
RATE_LIMIT_PER_MINUTE=100

Load with:

# Using python-dotenv
pip install python-dotenv

# Add to main.py
from dotenv import load_dotenv
load_dotenv()

docs.title: Configuration Inheritance
docs.text:
You can have multiple configuration files for different environments:

settings.json           # Base settings
settings.dev.json       # Development overrides
settings.prod.json      # Production overrides

docs.title: Programmatic Configuration
docs.text:
Override settings in code:

from config import Settings

# Create custom settings
settings = Settings(
    api_keys="custom-key",
    rate_limit_enabled=True
)

docs.title: Protecting Sensitive Data
docs.text:
1. Never commit settings.json to version control
  2. Use strong API keys (32+ characters, random)
  3. Rotate keys regularly in production
  4. Use environment variables for CI/CD
  5. Restrict file permissions: chmod 600 settings.json

docs.title: API Key Management
docs.text:
# Generate strong random key
python -c "import secrets; print(secrets.token_urlsafe(32))"

# Multiple keys for different clients
{
  "api_keys": "client1-key,client2-key,admin-key"
}

docs.title: Rate Limiting
docs.text:
{
  "rate_limit_enabled": true,
  "rate_limit_per_minute": 60,         // Public: 60 req/min
  "rate_limit_per_minute_authenticated": 300  // Authenticated: 300 req/min
}

docs.title: Configuration Reference
docs.text: See Configuration Options Reference for complete details on all configuration options.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: Get Apiary up and running on your system.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python 3.11+ installed
  • uv package manager (recommended) or pip
  • Git for cloning the repository

docs.title: Quick Install
docs.text:
# Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/lancereinsmith/apiary.git
cd apiary

# Install dependencies
uv sync

# Create configuration
cp settings_template.json settings.json
cp config/endpoints_template.json config/endpoints.json

# Run the application
python main.py

Visit http://localhost:8000 to see your API running.

docs.title: Using uv (Recommended)
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/lancereinsmith/apiary.git
cd apiary
uv sync

docs.title: Using pip
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/lancereinsmith/apiary.git
cd apiary
python -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate  # On Windows: .venv\Scripts\activate
pip install -r requirements.txt

docs.title: As a Template
docs.text:
Use Apiary as a GitHub template:

  1. Visit the Apiary repository
  2. Click "Use this template"
  3. Create your new repository
  4. Clone and set up:
git clone https://github.com/yourusername/your-api.git
cd your-api
uv sync
cp settings_template.json settings.json

Edit settings.json:

{
  "api_keys": "your-secret-key-here",
  "enable_landing_page": true,

  "rate_limit_per_minute_authenticated": 300
}

Edit config/endpoints.json to configure dynamic endpoints:

{
  "endpoints": [
    {
      "path": "/api/crypto",
      "method": "GET",
      "service": "crypto",
      "enabled": true,
      "requires_auth": false,

docs.title: Run the Application

Expected output:

INFO:     Uvicorn running on http://127.0.0.1:8000

docs.text:

curl http://localhost:8000/health

# API documentation
open http://localhost:8000/docs

docs.title: Run Tests
docs.text:

For development work:

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: Get up and running with Apiary in minutes! This guide will walk you through creating your first API endpoint.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Make sure you've completed the Installation guide.

docs.title: Start the Server
docs.text:
First, start the development server:

python main.py

You should see:

INFO:     Uvicorn running on http://127.0.0.1:8000

Visit http://localhost:8000 - you should see the landing page.

docs.title: Explore Built-in Endpoints
docs.text: Apiary comes with several built-in endpoints:

docs.title: Health Check
docs.text:
curl http://localhost:8000/health

Response:

{
  "status": "healthy",
  "timestamp": "2024-01-01T12:00:00.000000"
}

docs.title: Endpoint Discovery
docs.text:
curl http://localhost:8000/endpoints

This shows all available configurable endpoints and services.

docs.title: API Documentation
docs.text:
Visit these URLs in your browser:

  • Swagger UI: http://localhost:8000/docs
  • ReDoc: http://localhost:8000/redoc

docs.title: Create Your First Endpoint (Configuration-Based)
docs.text: The easiest way to add an endpoint is through configuration.

docs.title: Step 1: Understand the Example
docs.text:
Apiary includes a sample crypto service. Let's look at the configuration in config/endpoints.json:

{
  "endpoints": [
    {
      "path": "/api/crypto",
      "method": "GET",
      "service": "crypto",
      "enabled": true,
      "requires_auth": false,
      "description": "Get cryptocurrency price data",
      "tags": ["crypto"]
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Step 2: Test the Example Endpoint
docs.text:
# Get Bitcoin price (default)
curl http://localhost:8000/api/crypto

# Get Ethereum price
curl http://localhost:8000/api/crypto?symbol=ETH

# Get Solana price
curl http://localhost:8000/api/crypto?symbol=SOL

docs.title: Step 3: Add Your Own Endpoint
docs.text:
Edit config/endpoints.json to add a new endpoint:

{
  "endpoints": [
    {
      "path": "/api/crypto",
      "method": "GET",
      "service": "crypto",
      "enabled": true,
      "requires_auth": false,
      "description": "Get cryptocurrency price data",
      "tags": ["crypto"]
    },
    {
      "path": "/api/hello",
      "method": "GET",
      "service": "crypto",
      "enabled": true,
      "requires_auth": false,
      "description": "Hello world endpoint",
      "tags": ["demo"]
    }
  ]
}

Note

You'll need to restart the server for configuration changes to take effect.

docs.title: Step 4: Restart and Test
docs.text:
# Restart the server (Ctrl+C then python main.py)
python main.py

# Test your new endpoint
curl http://localhost:8000/api/hello

docs.title: Create a Custom Service
docs.text: Now let's create a custom service for your endpoint.

docs.title: Step 1: Create Service File
docs.text:
Create services/hello_service.py:

"""Hello world service."""

from typing import Any, Dict, Optional
from core.services.base import BaseService


class HelloService(BaseService):
    """Simple hello world service."""

    async def call(self, parameters: Optional[Dict[str, Any]] = None) -> Dict[str, Any]:
        """Return a hello message.

        Args:
            parameters: Request parameters (name optional)

        Returns:
            Dict with hello message
        """
        parameters = parameters or {}
        name = parameters.get("name", "World")

        return {
            "message": f"Hello, {name}!",
            "timestamp": self.get_timestamp()
        }

    def get_timestamp(self) -> str:
        """Get current timestamp."""
        from datetime import datetime
        return datetime.utcnow().isoformat()

docs.title: Step 2: Register the Service
docs.text:
Edit services/__init__.py to register your service:

"""Services package initialization."""

from core.services import register_service

# Import and register services
from services.crypto_service import CryptoService
from services.hello_service import HelloService

# Register services by name
register_service("crypto", CryptoService)
register_service("hello", HelloService)

__all__ = ["CryptoService", "HelloService"]

docs.title: Step 3: Update Endpoint Configuration
docs.text:
Update config/endpoints.json:

{
  "endpoints": [
    {
      "path": "/api/hello",
      "method": "GET",
      "service": "hello",
      "enabled": true,
      "requires_auth": false,
      "description": "Hello world endpoint. Accepts optional 'name' parameter.",
      "tags": ["demo"],
      "parameters": {
        "name": {
          "source": "query",
          "key": "name"
        }
      }
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Step 4: Test Your Service
docs.text:
Restart the server and test:

# Default hello
curl http://localhost:8000/api/hello

# Hello with name
curl http://localhost:8000/api/hello?name=Alice

Response:

{
  "message": "Hello, Alice!",
  "timestamp": "2024-01-01T12:00:00.000000"
}

docs.title: Add Authentication
docs.text: Let's protect an endpoint with API key authentication.

docs.title: Step 1: Configure API Keys
docs.text:
Edit settings.json:

{
  "api_keys": "secret-key-123,another-key-456",
  "enable_landing_page": true,
  "rate_limit_enabled": true,
  "rate_limit_per_minute": 60,
  "rate_limit_per_minute_authenticated": 300
}

docs.title: Step 2: Create Protected Endpoint
docs.text:
Add to config/endpoints.json:

{
  "path": "/api/hello/private",
  "method": "GET",
  "service": "hello",
  "enabled": true,
  "requires_auth": true,
  "description": "Protected hello endpoint (requires authentication)",
  "tags": ["demo"]
}

docs.title: Step 3: Test Authentication
docs.text:
# Without API key (should fail)
curl http://localhost:8000/api/hello/private

# With valid API key (should succeed)
curl -H "X-API-Key: secret-key-123" http://localhost:8000/api/hello/private

docs.title: Adding Endpoints
docs.text: This guide explains how to add new endpoints to your Apiary API using both code-based and configuration-based approaches.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Apiary supports two methods for creating endpoints:

  1. Code-based - Traditional FastAPI approach with full control
  2. Configuration-based - JSON configuration for rapid development

docs.title: Use Code-Based Endpoints When
docs.text:
• ✅ Complex business logic required
  • ✅ Custom validation needed
  • ✅ Special error handling
  • ✅ Multiple service orchestration
  • ✅ Custom response formatting

docs.title: Use Configuration-Based Endpoints When
docs.text:
• ✅ Simple service call
  • ✅ Rapid prototyping
  • ✅ Enable/disable without deployment
  • ✅ Standard request/response pattern
  • ✅ Non-technical configuration

docs.title: Basic Endpoint
docs.text:
Create a simple endpoint in a router file:

"""Example router."""

import fastapi

router = fastapi.APIRouter(tags=["example"])

@router.get("/hello")
async def hello():
    """Simple hello endpoint."""
    return {"message": "Hello, World!"}

docs.title: Endpoint with Dependencies
docs.text:
Use dependency injection for HTTP clients, settings, etc.:

from fastapi import Depends
import httpx
from core.dependencies import http_client_dependency
from config import Settings, get_settings

@router.get("/data")
async def get_data(
    client: httpx.AsyncClient = Depends(http_client_dependency),
    settings: Settings = Depends(get_settings),
):
    """Fetch data from external API."""
    response = await client.get("https://api.example.com/data")
    return response.json()

docs.title: Endpoint with Query Parameters
docs.text:
from pydantic import BaseModel

class QueryParams(BaseModel):
    """Query parameters model."""
    name: str
    count: int = 10

@router.get("/greet")
async def greet(params: QueryParams = Depends()):
    """Greet endpoint with query parameters."""
    return {
        "message": f"Hello, {params.name}!",
        "count": params.count,
    }

Usage: GET /greet?name=Alice&count=5

docs.title: Protected Endpoint
docs.text:
Require authentication using the require_auth dependency:

from core.auth.authorization import require_auth
from core.auth.authentication import AuthenticatedUser

@router.get("/protected")
async def protected_endpoint(
    user: AuthenticatedUser = Depends(require_auth),
):
    """Protected endpoint requiring API key."""
    return {
        "message": "You are authenticated",
        "api_key": user.api_key,
    }

docs.title: Response Models
docs.text:
Define response models for better documentation and validation:

from pydantic import BaseModel
from models.responses import BaseResponse

class DataResponse(BaseResponse):
    """Response model for data endpoint."""
    data: dict
    count: int

@router.get("/data", response_model=DataResponse)
async def get_data() -> DataResponse:
    """Get data with typed response."""
    return DataResponse(
        data={"key": "value"},
        count=1,
    )

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
Use custom exceptions from core.exceptions:

from core import ValidationError, NotFoundError

@router.get("/user/{user_id}")
async def get_user(user_id: int):
    """Get user by ID."""
    user = await fetch_user(user_id)

    if not user:
        raise NotFoundError(f"User {user_id} not found")

    if not user.is_active:
        raise ValidationError("User is not active")

    return user

docs.title: Caching
docs.text:
Add cache headers to reduce external API calls:

from core.cache import add_cache_headers
from fastapi import Response

@router.get("/cached")
async def cached_endpoint(response: Response):
    """Endpoint with caching."""
    data = await expensive_operation()

    # Cache for 60 seconds
    add_cache_headers(response, ttl=60)

    return data

docs.title: Register Router
docs.text:
Add your router to main.py:

from routers import example

def configure_routing():
    """Configure application routes."""
    api.include_router(example.router)

docs.title: Configuration-Based Endpoints
docs.text: Configuration-based endpoints allow you to add endpoints without writing code.

docs.title: Step 1: Create a Service
docs.text:
If you need a new service, create it in services/:

"""Example service."""

from typing import Any, Dict, Optional
from core.services.base import BaseService

class ExampleService(BaseService):
    """Example service for demonstration."""

    async def call(
        self, 
        parameters: Optional[Dict[str, Any]] = None
    ) -> Dict[str, Any]:
        """Execute the service.

        Args:
            parameters: Request parameters

        Returns:
            Service response
        """
        parameters = parameters or {}
        name = parameters.get("name", "World")

        return {
            "message": f"Hello, {name}!",
            "timestamp": self._get_timestamp(),
        }

    def _get_timestamp(self) -> str:
        """Get current timestamp."""
        from datetime import datetime
        return datetime.utcnow().isoformat()

docs.title: Step 2: Register Service
docs.text:
Register in services/__init__.py:

from core.services import register_service
from services.example_service import ExampleService

register_service("example", ExampleService)

docs.title: Step 3: Add Endpoint Configuration
docs.text:
Edit config/endpoints.json:

{
  "endpoints": [
    {
      "path": "/api/example",
      "method": "GET",
      "service": "example",
      "enabled": true,
      "requires_auth": false,
      "description": "Example endpoint",
      "tags": ["example"],
      "summary": "Get example data"
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Step 4: Test
docs.text:
Restart the server and test:

curl http://localhost:8000/api/example

docs.title: Parameter Mapping
docs.text: Configuration-based endpoints support parameter mapping.

docs.title: Query Parameters
docs.text:
Map query parameters to service parameters:

{
  "path": "/api/greet",
  "method": "GET",
  "service": "greet",
  "enabled": true,
  "requires_auth": false,
  "parameters": {
    "name": {
      "source": "query",
      "key": "name"
    }
  }
}

Usage: GET /api/greet?name=Alice

docs.title: Path Parameters
docs.text:
Map path parameters:

{
  "path": "/api/user/{id}",
  "method": "GET",
  "service": "user",
  "enabled": true,
  "requires_auth": false,
  "parameters": {
    "user_id": {
      "source": "path",
      "key": "id"
    }
  }
}

Usage: GET /api/user/123

docs.title: Static Values
docs.text:
Provide static values:

{
  "path": "/api/example",
  "method": "GET",
  "service": "example",
  "enabled": true,
  "requires_auth": false,
  "parameters": {
    "version": "v1",
    "source": "api"
  }
}

docs.title: Example 1: Weather Service
docs.text:
Create services/weather_service.py:

from typing import Any, Dict, Optional
from core.services.base import BaseService
from core import ValidationError
import httpx

class WeatherService(BaseService):
    """Weather data service."""

    async def call(
        self, 
        parameters: Optional[Dict[str, Any]] = None
    ) -> Dict[str, Any]:
        """Get weather data.

        Args:
            parameters: Must include 'city'

        Returns:
            Weather data
        """
        parameters = parameters or {}
        city = parameters.get("city")

        if not city:
            raise ValidationError("City parameter required")

        # Call weather API
        async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
            response = await client.get(
                f"https://api.weatherapi.com/v1/current.json",
                params={"q": city, "key": "YOUR_API_KEY"}
            )

            if response.status_code != 200:
                raise ValidationError("Failed to fetch weather data")

            data = response.json()

            return {
                "city": city,
                "temperature": data["current"]["temp_c"],
                "condition": data["current"]["condition"]["text"],
            }

Register and configure:

# services/__init__.py
register_service("weather", WeatherService)

{
  "path": "/api/weather",
  "method": "GET",
  "service": "weather",
  "enabled": true,
  "requires_auth": false,
  "description": "Get current weather for a city",
  "tags": ["weather"],
  "parameters": {
    "city": {
      "source": "query",
      "key": "city"
    }
  }
}

Usage: GET /api/weather?city=London

docs.title: Example 2: Protected Admin Endpoint
docs.text:
Code-based approach:

@router.get("/admin/stats")
async def admin_stats(
    user: AuthenticatedUser = Depends(require_auth),
):
    """Admin statistics endpoint."""
    return {
        "total_requests": get_total_requests(),
        "active_users": get_active_users(),
        "system_health": get_system_health(),
    }

Configuration-based approach:

{
  "path": "/api/admin/stats",
  "method": "GET",
  "service": "admin_stats",
  "enabled": true,
  "requires_auth": true,
  "description": "Admin statistics (requires authentication)",
  "tags": ["admin"]
}

docs.title: Code-Based Endpoints
docs.text:
1. Use response models for type safety
  2. Add comprehensive documentation in docstrings
  3. Handle errors explicitly with custom exceptions
  4. Use dependency injection for testability
  5. Add cache headers for expensive operations
  6. Log important events for debugging

docs.title: Configuration-Based Endpoints
docs.text:
1. Keep services simple and focused
  2. Validate parameters in service
  3. Use clear, descriptive paths
  4. Document in configuration (description, summary)
  5. Test thoroughly after adding
  6. Version your APIs if needed

docs.title: Both Approaches
docs.text:
1. Test your endpoints with unit and integration tests
  2. Monitor performance using metrics
  3. Use semantic HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE)
  4. Return appropriate status codes (200, 400, 404, 500)
  5. Validate all input before processing
  6. Document authentication requirements clearly

docs.title: Endpoint Not Appearing
docs.text:
Code-based:

  • Verify router is registered in main.py
  • Check for syntax errors
  • Restart the server

Configuration-based:

  • Check JSON syntax is valid
  • Verify service is registered
  • Ensure enabled: true
  • Check logs for errors
  • Restart the server

docs.title: Authentication Not Working
docs.text:
• Verify api_keys in settings.json
  • Check requires_auth setting
  • Ensure X-API-Key header is sent
  • Check logs for auth errors

docs.title: Parameters Not Working
docs.text:
• Verify parameter mapping in configuration
  • Check parameter source (query/path) matches request
  • Ensure parameter keys match exactly
  • Test with curl to isolate issues

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Creating Services - Build robust services
  • Authentication - Secure your endpoints
  • Built-in Endpoints - Use what's included
  • Testing - Write tests for endpoints

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text: Learn how to implement authentication and authorization in your Apiary API.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Apiary uses API key authentication via the X-API-Key header. This provides:

  • ✅ Simple implementation
  • ✅ No session management
  • ✅ Stateless authentication
  • ✅ Easy to test
  • ✅ Per-endpoint control

docs.title: Setting API Keys
docs.text:
Edit settings.json:

{
  "api_keys": "key1,key2,key3"
}

Multiple keys can be comma-separated. Each key can represent a different client or user.

docs.title: Generating Secure Keys
docs.text:
Generate strong random keys:

python -c "import secrets; print(secrets.token_urlsafe(32))"

Example output: xvT5_3kP9mN2wQ8rL6hJ4fD1sA7yU0bC5eK9nM3pR2t

docs.title: Making Authenticated Requests
docs.text:
Include the X-API-Key header:

curl -H "X-API-Key: your-key-here" http://localhost:8000/auth/status

docs.title: In Python
docs.text:
import httpx

headers = {"X-API-Key": "your-key-here"}
response = httpx.get("http://localhost:8000/auth/status", headers=headers)
print(response.json())

docs.title: In JavaScript
docs.text:
fetch('http://localhost:8000/auth/status', {
  headers: {
    'X-API-Key': 'your-key-here'
  }
})
.then(response => response.json())
.then(data => console.log(data));

docs.title: Code-Based Endpoints
docs.text:
Use the require_auth dependency:

from fastapi import Depends
from core.auth.authorization import require_auth
from core.auth.authentication import AuthenticatedUser

@router.get("/protected")
async def protected_endpoint(
    user: AuthenticatedUser = Depends(require_auth)
):
    """Protected endpoint requiring API key."""
    return {
        "message": "You are authenticated",
        "api_key": user.api_key,
    }

docs.title: Configuration-Based Endpoints
docs.text:
Set requires_auth: true:

{
  "path": "/api/protected",
  "method": "GET",
  "service": "myservice",
  "enabled": true,
  "requires_auth": true
}

docs.title: Check Authentication Status
docs.text:
# Requires authentication
curl -H "X-API-Key: your-key" http://localhost:8000/auth/status

Response:

{
  "authenticated": true,
  "api_key": "your-key"
}

docs.title: Validate API Key
docs.text:
# Optional authentication
curl -H "X-API-Key: your-key" http://localhost:8000/auth/validate

Response:

{
  "authenticated": true,
  "valid": true
}

docs.title: Rate Limiting
docs.text:
Authenticated users get higher rate limits:

{
  "rate_limit_enabled": true,
  "rate_limit_per_minute": 60,                    // Public
  "rate_limit_per_minute_authenticated": 300      // Authenticated
}

docs.title: 1. Use Strong Keys
docs.text:
# Generate 32-byte random key
python -c "import secrets; print(secrets.token_urlsafe(32))"

docs.title: 2. Rotate Keys Regularly
docs.text:
Update keys periodically in production:

{
  "api_keys": "new-key-1,new-key-2,old-key-1"
}

Keep old keys temporarily for transition.

docs.title: 3. Different Keys for Different Clients
docs.text:
{
  "api_keys": "mobile-app-key,web-app-key,admin-key"
}

docs.title: 4. Never Commit Keys
docs.text:
Add to .gitignore:

settings.json
config/endpoints.json

docs.title: 5. Use Environment Variables in Production
docs.text: export API_KEYS="prod-key-1,prod-key-2"

docs.title: HTTPS in Production
docs.text: Always use HTTPS in production to protect API keys in transit.

docs.title: Key Storage
docs.text:
• ❌ Don't hardcode in client applications
  • ❌ Don't commit to version control
  • ✅ Use environment variables
  • ✅ Use secret management services
  • ✅ Restrict file permissions: chmod 600 settings.json

docs.title: Monitoring
docs.text:
Log authentication failures:

logger.warning(
    "Authentication failed",
    extra={"ip": request.client.host}
)

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Built-in Endpoints - Explore auth endpoints
  • Adding Endpoints - Create protected endpoints
  • Deployment - Production security

docs.title: Built-in Endpoints
docs.text: Apiary includes several built-in endpoints for health checks, metrics, authentication, and endpoint discovery.

docs.title: Basic Health Check
docs.text:
Endpoint: GET /health

Simple health check that returns the service status.

curl http://localhost:8000/health

Response:

{
  "status": "healthy",
  "timestamp": "2024-01-01T12:00:00.000000"
}

docs.title: Liveness Probe
docs.text:
Endpoint: GET /health/live

Kubernetes-compatible liveness probe. Returns 200 if the application is running.

curl http://localhost:8000/health/live

Response:

{
  "status": "alive"
}

docs.title: Readiness Probe
docs.text:
Endpoint: GET /health/ready

Kubernetes-compatible readiness probe. Checks if the application and its dependencies are ready to serve traffic.

curl http://localhost:8000/health/ready

Response (healthy):

{
  "status": "ready",
  "dependencies": {
    "external_apis": "healthy"
  },
  "uptime_seconds": 3600
}

Response (degraded):

{
  "status": "degraded",
  "dependencies": {
    "external_apis": "unhealthy"
  },
  "uptime_seconds": 3600
}

docs.title: Metrics Endpoint
docs.text:
Endpoint: GET /metrics

Returns application metrics including request counts, error rates, and endpoint statistics.

curl http://localhost:8000/metrics

Response:

{
  "total_requests": 1234,
  "total_errors": 5,
  "error_rate": 0.004,
  "endpoints": {
    "/api/crypto": {
      "count": 456,
      "avg_duration_ms": 123.45,
      "errors": 2
    }
  },
  "uptime_seconds": 86400
}

docs.title: Validate API Key
docs.text:
Endpoint: GET /auth/validate

Validates an API key without requiring authentication.

# Without key
curl http://localhost:8000/auth/validate

# With key
curl -H "X-API-Key: your-key" http://localhost:8000/auth/validate

Response:

{
  "authenticated": true,
  "valid": true
}

docs.title: Authentication Status
docs.text:
Endpoint: GET /auth/status

Requires authentication

Returns the current authentication status.

curl -H "X-API-Key: your-key" http://localhost:8000/auth/status

Response:

{
  "authenticated": true,
  "api_key": "your-key"
}

docs.title: Endpoint Discovery
docs.text:
Endpoint: GET /endpoints

Lists all configurable endpoints and available services.

curl http://localhost:8000/endpoints

Response:

{
  "endpoints": [
    {
      "path": "/api/crypto",
      "method": "GET",
      "service": "crypto",
      "enabled": true,
      "requires_auth": false,
      "description": "Get cryptocurrency price data"
    }
  ],
  "services": ["crypto", "weather"],
  "total": 1
}

docs.title: Swagger UI
docs.text:
Endpoint: GET /docs

Interactive API documentation with Swagger UI.

Visit: http://localhost:8000/docs

docs.title: ReDoc
docs.text:
Endpoint: GET /redoc

Alternative API documentation with ReDoc.

Visit: http://localhost:8000/redoc

docs.title: OpenAPI JSON
docs.text:
Endpoint: GET /openapi.json

Raw OpenAPI specification in JSON format.

curl http://localhost:8000/openapi.json

docs.title: Landing Page
docs.text:
Endpoint: GET /

HTML landing page (if enabled in settings).

Visit: http://localhost:8000/

Can be disabled in settings.json:

{
  "enable_landing_page": false
}

docs.title: Rate Limiting Headers
docs.text:
All endpoints include rate limiting headers:

X-RateLimit-Limit: 60
X-RateLimit-Remaining: 59
X-RateLimit-Reset: 1234567890

docs.title: Health Checks in Kubernetes
docs.text:
livenessProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /health/live
    port: 8000
  initialDelaySeconds: 10
  periodSeconds: 30

readinessProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /health/ready
    port: 8000
  initialDelaySeconds: 5
  periodSeconds: 10

docs.title: Monitoring with Prometheus
docs.text:
Configure Prometheus to scrape /metrics:

scrape_configs:
  - job_name: 'apiary'
    static_configs:
      - targets: ['localhost:8000']
    metrics_path: '/metrics'

docs.title: CI/CD Health Checks
docs.text:
#!/bin/bash
# Wait for service to be ready
until curl -f http://localhost:8000/health/ready; do
  echo "Waiting for service..."
  sleep 5
done
echo "Service is ready!"

docs.title: Configurable Endpoints
docs.text: Master Apiary's configuration-driven endpoint system to add and manage endpoints without code changes.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Configurable endpoints allow you to:

  • ✅ Add endpoints via JSON configuration
  • ✅ Enable/disable endpoints without deployment
  • ✅ Rapid prototyping and iteration
  • ✅ Non-technical endpoint management
  • ✅ Consistent endpoint patterns

docs.title: Configuration File
docs.text:
Endpoints are defined in config/endpoints.json:

{
  "endpoints": [
    {
      "path": "/api/example",
      "method": "GET",
      "service": "example",
      "enabled": true,
      "requires_auth": false,
      "description": "Example endpoint",
      "tags": ["example"]
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Complete Configuration Options
docs.text:
{
  "path": "/api/endpoint",
  "method": "GET",
  "service": "myservice",
  "enabled": true,
  "requires_auth": false,
  "description": "Detailed description for API docs",
  "tags": ["category"],
  "summary": "Short summary",
  "parameters": {
    "param1": "static-value",
    "param2": {
      "source": "query",
      "key": "q"
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Static Values
docs.text:
{
  "parameters": {
    "version": "v1",
    "source": "api"
  }
}

docs.title: Query Parameters
docs.text:
{
  "parameters": {
    "city": {
      "source": "query",
      "key": "city"
    }
  }
}

Usage: GET /api/weather?city=London

docs.title: Path Parameters
docs.text:
{
  "path": "/api/user/{id}",
  "parameters": {
    "user_id": {
      "source": "path",
      "key": "id"
    }

Usage: GET /api/user/123

  "path": "/api/search/{category}",

docs.title: 1. Use Clear Paths
docs.text:
// Good
{"path": "/api/crypto"}

docs.title: Endpoint Not Appearing
docs.text:
1. Check JSON syntax: python -m json.tool config/endpoints.json
  2. Verify service is registered
  3. Ensure enabled: true
  4. Restart the server
  5. Check logs for errors

docs.title: Service Not Found
docs.text:
1. Verify service registration in services/__init__.py
  2. Check service name matches exactly
  3. Ensure service inherits from BaseService

docs.title: Parameters Not Working
docs.text:
1. Verify parameter mapping syntax
  2. Check source type (query/path)
  3. Ensure keys match exactly

docs.title: Creating Services
docs.text: Learn how to create reusable services for your Apiary API.

docs.title: What is a Service?
docs.text:
A service encapsulates business logic that can be called by endpoints. Services:

  • ✅ Contain business logic
  • ✅ Can be reused by multiple endpoints
  • ✅ Are testable in isolation
  • ✅ Support dependency injection
  • ✅ Work with configurable endpoints

docs.title: Base Service Interface
docs.text:
All services should inherit from BaseService:

from typing import Any, Dict, Optional
from core.services.base import BaseService

class MyService(BaseService):
    """My custom service."""

    async def call(
        self, 
        parameters: Optional[Dict[str, Any]] = None
    ) -> Dict[str, Any]:
        """Execute the service.

        Args:
            parameters: Request parameters

        Returns:
            Service response dictionary
        """
        parameters = parameters or {}

        # Your business logic here
        result = await self._do_something(parameters)

        return {"result": result}

docs.title: Step 1: Create Service File
docs.text:
Create a new file in services/:

"""Weather service."""

from typing import Any, Dict, Optional
import httpx
from core.services.base import BaseService
from core import ValidationError

class WeatherService(BaseService):
    """Service for fetching weather data."""

    async def call(
        self, 
        parameters: Optional[Dict[str, Any]] = None
    ) -> Dict[str, Any]:
        """Get weather for a city.

        Args:
            parameters: Must include 'city' key

        Returns:
            Weather data dictionary

        Raises:
            ValidationError: If city not provided or API fails
        """
        parameters = parameters or {}
        city = parameters.get("city")

        if not city:
            raise ValidationError("City parameter is required")

        # Fetch weather data
        weather_data = await self._fetch_weather(city)

        return {
            "city": city,
            "temperature": weather_data["temp"],
            "condition": weather_data["condition"],
        }

    async def _fetch_weather(self, city: str) -> Dict[str, Any]:
        """Fetch weather from external API."""
        async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
            response = await client.get(
                f"https://api.weatherapi.com/v1/current.json",
                params={"q": city}
            )

            if response.status_code != 200:
                raise ValidationError(f"Failed to fetch weather for {city}")

            data = response.json()
            return {
                "temp": data["current"]["temp_c"],
                "condition": data["current"]["condition"]["text"],
            }

docs.title: Step 2: Register Service
docs.text:
Add to services/__init__.py:

from core.services import register_service
from services.weather_service import WeatherService

register_service("weather", WeatherService)

docs.title: Step 3: Use in Endpoint
docs.text:
Configuration-based:

{
  "path": "/api/weather",
  "method": "GET",
  "service": "weather",
  "enabled": true,
  "parameters": {
    "city": {
      "source": "query",
      "key": "city"
    }
  }
}

Code-based:

@router.get("/weather")
async def get_weather(city: str):
    service = WeatherService()
    result = await service.call({"city": city})
    return result

docs.title: Simple Data Service
docs.text:
class DataService(BaseService):
    """Simple data retrieval service."""

    async def call(self, parameters=None):
        return {
            "data": "Hello, World!",
            "timestamp": self._get_timestamp()
        }

docs.title: External API Service
docs.text:
class APIService(BaseService):
    """Service that calls external API."""

    async def call(self, parameters=None):
        parameters = parameters or {}
        endpoint = parameters.get("endpoint", "default")

        async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
            response = await client.get(
                f"https://api.example.com/{endpoint}"
            )
            return response.json()

docs.title: Database Service
docs.text:
class DatabaseService(BaseService):
    """Service that queries database."""

    async def call(self, parameters=None):
        parameters = parameters or {}
        user_id = parameters.get("user_id")

        # Query database
        user = await self._get_user(user_id)

        return {
            "id": user.id,
            "name": user.name,
            "email": user.email,
        }

docs.title: Aggregation Service
docs.text:
class AggregationService(BaseService):
    """Service that combines multiple data sources."""

    async def call(self, parameters=None):
        # Call multiple services
        weather = await WeatherService().call({"city": "London"})
        news = await NewsService().call({"category": "tech"})

        return {
            "weather": weather,
            "news": news,
        }

docs.title: 1. Error Handling
docs.text:
Always handle errors appropriately:

from core import ValidationError, NotFoundError

async def call(self, parameters=None):
    try:
        result = await self._fetch_data()
    except httpx.HTTPError as e:
        raise ValidationError(f"API error: {str(e)}")
    except KeyError as e:
        raise NotFoundError(f"Data not found: {str(e)}")

    return result

docs.title: 2. Input Validation
docs.text:
Validate all inputs:

async def call(self, parameters=None):
    parameters = parameters or {}

    # Validate required parameters
    if "user_id" not in parameters:
        raise ValidationError("user_id is required")

    # Validate parameter types
    user_id = parameters["user_id"]
    if not isinstance(user_id, int):
        raise ValidationError("user_id must be an integer")

    # Continue with logic
    return await self._process(user_id)

docs.title: 3. Dependency Injection
docs.text:
Use constructor injection for dependencies:

class MyService(BaseService):
    """Service with dependencies."""

    def __init__(self, http_client: httpx.AsyncClient = None):
        super().__init__()
        self.http_client = http_client or httpx.AsyncClient()

    async def call(self, parameters=None):
        response = await self.http_client.get("https://api.example.com")
        return response.json()

docs.title: 4. Logging
docs.text:
Add logging for debugging:

import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

class MyService(BaseService):
    async def call(self, parameters=None):
        logger.info("Service called", extra={"parameters": parameters})

        try:
            result = await self._process(parameters)
            logger.info("Service completed successfully")
            return result
        except Exception as e:
            logger.error(f"Service failed: {str(e)}")
            raise

docs.title: 5. Testing
docs.text:
Write unit tests for services:

import pytest
from services.weather_service import WeatherService

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_weather_service():
    service = WeatherService()
    result = await service.call({"city": "London"})

    assert "city" in result
    assert result["city"] == "London"
    assert "temperature" in result

docs.title: User Guide Overview
docs.text: Welcome to the Apiary User Guide! This guide covers everything you need to know to build production-ready APIs with Apiary.

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text: This guide is organized into the following sections:

docs.title: Core Concepts
docs.text:
1. Adding Endpoints - Learn how to create API endpoints
  2. Code-based endpoints (traditional FastAPI)
  3. Configuration-based endpoints (Apiary's unique feature)

  4. When to use each approach

  5. Creating Services - Build reusable business logic

  6. Service architecture
  7. Base service interface

  8. Best practices for services

  9. Authentication - Secure your API

  10. API key authentication
  11. Public vs protected endpoints
  12. Authorization patterns

docs.text:
1. Built-in Endpoints - Understand what's included
  2. Health checks
  3. Metrics

  5. Authentication endpoints

  6. Configurable Endpoints - Master the configuration system

  7. Endpoint configuration format

docs.title: Apiary Architecture
docs.text: Understanding Apiary's architecture will help you make the most of its features.

docs.title: Request Flow
docs.text:
1. Request arrives at FastAPI
  2. Middleware processes request (logging, rate limiting, validation)
  3. Router dispatches to appropriate handler
  4. Service executes business logic
  5. Response returned with proper formatting

docs.title: 1. Separation of Concerns
docs.text:
• Routers: Handle HTTP requests/responses
  • Services: Contain business logic
  • Models: Define data structures
  • Middleware: Cross-cutting concerns

docs.title: 2. Dependency Injection
docs.text:
Everything uses FastAPI's dependency injection:

@router.get("/endpoint")
async def endpoint(
    client: httpx.AsyncClient = Depends(http_client_dependency),
    settings: Settings = Depends(get_settings),
):
    # Use injected dependencies
    pass

docs.title: 3. Configuration-Driven
docs.text:
Much of the behavior can be controlled via configuration:

  • Endpoints can be added without code
  • Features can be enabled/disabled
  • Rate limits are configurable
  • Services are pluggable

docs.title: Two Approaches to Endpoints
docs.text: Apiary supports two ways to create endpoints. Choose based on your needs:

docs.title: Code-Based Endpoints
docs.text:
Best for:

  • Complex logic
  • Custom validation
  • Special error handling
  • Direct control

Example:

@router.get("/complex")
async def complex_endpoint(
    param: str,
    client: httpx.AsyncClient = Depends(http_client_dependency),
):
    # Complex custom logic
    result = await do_something_complex()
    return result

docs.title: Configuration-Based Endpoints
docs.text:
Best for:

  • Simple service calls
  • Rapid prototyping
  • Enabling/disabling features
  • No code deployments

Example:

{
  "path": "/api/crypto",
  "method": "GET",
  "service": "crypto",
  "enabled": true
}

docs.title: Public Endpoint
docs.text:
Accessible without authentication:

@router.get("/public")
async def public_endpoint():
    return {"message": "Public data"}

Or in configuration:

{
  "path": "/api/public",
  "requires_auth": false
}

docs.title: Protected Endpoint
docs.text:
Requires API key:

@router.get("/protected")
async def protected_endpoint(
    user: AuthenticatedUser = Depends(require_auth)
):
    return {"message": "Protected data"}

Or in configuration:

{
  "path": "/api/protected",
  "requires_auth": true
}

docs.title: Cached Endpoint
docs.text:
Add caching to reduce load:

from core.cache import add_cache_headers

@router.get("/cached")
async def cached_endpoint(response: Response):
    data = expensive_operation()
    add_cache_headers(response, ttl=60)  # Cache 60s
    return data

docs.title: Aggregation Endpoint
docs.text:
Combine multiple services:

@router.get("/combined")
async def combined_endpoint(
    client: httpx.AsyncClient = Depends(http_client_dependency)
):
    crypto = await crypto_service.call()
    weather = await weather_service.call()

    return {
        "crypto": crypto,
        "weather": weather
    }

docs.text:

docs.text:

  • Use BaseService for configurable services

docs.title: 3. Add Endpoints
docs.text:
Choose your approach:

  • Simple: Use configuration
  • Complex: Write code-based endpoints

docs.text:

  • Configure endpoints.json
  • Set rate limits
  • Define API keys

docs.text:
• Run unit tests
  • Test endpoints with curl
  • Check metrics
  • Verify health checks

docs.text:
• Configure production settings
  • Set up nginx
  • Configure systemd
  • Monitor metrics

docs.title: Service Design
docs.text:
1. Keep services focused - One responsibility per service
  2. Use dependency injection - Don't create clients in services
  3. Handle errors properly - Use custom exceptions
  4. Make services async - Use async/await for I/O

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
1. Use custom exceptions - from core.exceptions
  2. Include helpful messages - For debugging
  3. Log errors - With appropriate log levels
  4. Return proper status codes - 400, 404, 500, etc.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
1. Use settings.json - For sensitive data
  2. Use environment variables - For deployment
  3. Validate on startup - Fail fast with clear errors
  4. Document settings - So others know what to configure

docs.title: Security
docs.text:
1. Use strong API keys - Random, long strings
  2. Enable rate limiting - Prevent abuse
  3. Validate input - Use Pydantic models
  4. Log security events - Authentication failures, etc.

docs.title: Performance
docs.text:
1. Enable caching - For expensive operations
  2. Use connection pooling - Reuse HTTP connections
  3. Monitor metrics - Track slow endpoints
  4. Optimize hot paths - Profile and improve

docs.text:

python main.py

# Run tests

  "api_keys": "key1,key2",

docs.title: Configuration Reference
docs.text: Complete reference for all configuration options.

docs.title: api_keys
docs.text:
Comma-separated list of valid API keys.

  • Type: String
  • Default: ""
  • Example: "key1,key2,key3"

docs.title: enable_landing_page
docs.text:
Enable/disable HTML landing page.

  • Type: Boolean
  • Default: true

docs.title: rate_limit_enabled
docs.text:
Enable/disable rate limiting.

  • Type: Boolean
  • Default: false

docs.title: rate_limit_per_minute
docs.text:
Rate limit for unauthenticated requests.

  • Type: Integer
  • Default: 60

docs.title: rate_limit_per_minute_authenticated
docs.text:
Rate limit for authenticated requests.

  • Type: Integer
  • Default: 300

docs.title: path
docs.text:
URL path for endpoint (must start with /).

  • Type: String
  • Required: Yes

docs.title: method
docs.text:
HTTP method.

  • Type: String
  • Values: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH
  • Required: Yes

docs.title: service
docs.text:
Service name (must be registered).

  • Type: String
  • Required: Yes

docs.title: enabled
docs.text:
Enable/disable endpoint.

  • Type: Boolean
  • Required: Yes

docs.title: requires_auth
docs.text:
Require authentication.

  • Type: Boolean
  • Required: Yes

docs.title: description
docs.text:
Detailed description for API docs.

  • Type: String
  • Required: No

docs.title: tags
docs.text:
OpenAPI tags for grouping.

  • Type: Array of strings
  • Required: No

docs.title: summary
docs.text:
Short summary for API docs.

  • Type: String
  • Required: No

docs.title: parameters
docs.text:
Parameter mapping.

  • Type: Object
  • Required: No
